parentQuestion: Glands whose secretions are stored and then released when the cell ruptures and dies are classified as ___.

scaffolds.q: Glands can release their secretions in different ways.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Some glands use gentle exocytosis, others tear off parts of themselves, and some literally explode.

scaffolds.q: There are THREE main modes of secretion: merocrine, apocrine, and holocrine.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Each mode describes HOW the cell releases its product - from gentle to destructive.

scaffolds.q: MEROCRINE secretion uses exocytosis - the cell stays intact.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Vesicles release contents at the cell surface. The cell survives and keeps working. Sweat glands work this way.

scaffolds.q: APOCRINE secretion pinches off part of the cell - partial loss.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The cell's apex breaks off with the secretion. The cell survives but loses a piece. Some mammary glands work this way.

scaffolds.q: HOLOCRINE secretion destroys the entire cell - the cell BECOMES the product.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! 'Holo-' means whole/entire. The whole cell fills with product, dies, and ruptures to release it.

scaffolds.q: Sebaceous glands (oil glands) use holocrine secretion.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The cell fills with sebum (oil), swells, and eventually ruptures. Cellular martyrdom for smooth skin.

scaffolds.q: In holocrine secretion, the cell dies as part of releasing its product.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Unlike merocrine (cell lives) or apocrine (cell loses apex), holocrine means total cell destruction.

scaffolds.q: Glands that release secretions when the cell RUPTURES and DIES are HOLOCRINE.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Rupture + cell death = holocrine. The entire cell becomes the secretion.

scaffolds.q: The answer is HOLOCRINE - cells fill with product, then rupture and die to release it.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not merocrine (cell stays intact), not apocrine (only apex pinches off). Holocrine = whole cell destruction.
